slides.title: Scientific Process Integration
slides.text:
Scientific research integrates multiple process skills to establish causal relationships and document empirical evidence.
This slide introduces key insights about Scientific Process Integration.
For instance, consider the example of Causal Relationship:
A link where one event (cause) makes another event happen (effect).
Another case is the study of Systematic Observation:
Observing with standardized protocols to eliminate human bias.
How does the concept of Scientific Process Integration help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Causal Relationship
examples.content: A link where one event (cause) makes another event happen (effect).

examples.title: Systematic Observation
examples.content: Observing with standardized protocols to eliminate human bias.

slides.title: Advanced Vocabulary
slides.text:
Terms of empirical research: Quantitative analysis, Extrapolation, Interpolation, and Correlation.
This slide introduces key insights about Advanced Vocabulary.
For instance, consider the example of Extrapolation:
Estimating values beyond the observed range based on a trend.
Another case is the study of Correlation:
A mutual relationship or connection between two variables.
How does the concept of Advanced Vocabulary help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Extrapolation
examples.content: Estimating values beyond the observed range based on a trend.

examples.title: Correlation
examples.content: A mutual relationship or connection between two variables.

slides.title: Empirical Reasoning
slides.text:
Scientists use process skills to construct models, communicate raw data tables, and formulate theories.
This slide introduces key insights about Empirical Reasoning.
For instance, consider the example of Scientific Theory:
A well-substantiated explanation of some aspect of the natural world.
Another case is the study of Model Construction:
Creating physical or digital representations to study systems.
How does the concept of Empirical Reasoning help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Scientific Theory
examples.content: A well-substantiated explanation of some aspect of the natural world.

examples.title: Model Construction
examples.content: Creating physical or digital representations to study systems.

slides.title: Process: Botanical Observation
slides.text:
Using high-magnification tools to observe plant guard cell behavior under different humidity levels.
This slide introduces key insights about Process: Botanical Observation.
For instance, consider the example of Stomata:
Microscopic pores on leaves that open and close to regulate gas exchange.
Another case is the study of Osmotic Pressure:
The force that drives water in and out of guard cells.
How does the concept of Process: Botanical Observation help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Stomata
examples.content: Microscopic pores on leaves that open and close to regulate gas exchange.

examples.title: Osmotic Pressure
examples.content: The force that drives water in and out of guard cells.

slides.title: Process: Buoyancy Predictions
slides.text:
Predicting displacement volume and buoyant forces using Archimedes' principle.
This slide introduces key insights about Process: Buoyancy Predictions.
For instance, consider the example of Archimedes Principle:
An object floats when the buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid.
Another case is the study of Volume Displacement:
Measuring exactly how much water is pushed aside in cubic centimeters.
How does the concept of Process: Buoyancy Predictions help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Archimedes Principle
examples.content: An object floats when the buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid.

examples.title: Volume Displacement
examples.content: Measuring exactly how much water is pushed aside in cubic centimeters.

slides.title: Process: Precise Calibration
slides.text:
Measuring liquid meniscus levels with graduated glassware, adjusting for thermal expansion of liquids.
This slide introduces key insights about Process: Precise Calibration.
For instance, consider the example of Cohesive Forces:
Attraction between similar molecules, pulling water into a curve.
Another case is the study of Thermal Expansion:
Liquids expand slightly when heated, changing volume readings.
How does the concept of Process: Precise Calibration help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Cohesive Forces
examples.content: Attraction between similar molecules, pulling water into a curve.

examples.title: Thermal Expansion
examples.content: Liquids expand slightly when heated, changing volume readings.

slides.title: Process: Taxonomic Classification
slides.text:
Classifying organisms using a dichotomous key based on binary morphological characteristics.
This slide introduces key insights about Process: Taxonomic Classification.
For instance, consider the example of Dichotomous Key:
A tool that allows the user to identify organisms through progressive choices.
Another case is the study of Morphology:
The physical shape and structure of an organism.
How does the concept of Process: Taxonomic Classification help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Dichotomous Key
examples.content: A tool that allows the user to identify organisms through progressive choices.

examples.title: Morphology
examples.content: The physical shape and structure of an organism.

slides.title: Process: Graphical Communication
slides.text:
Communicating multi-variable datasets using scatter plots, line graphs, and regression lines.
This slide introduces key insights about Process: Graphical Communication.
For instance, consider the example of Independent Axis:
The X-axis, representing the independent variable.
Another case is the study of Dependent Axis:
The Y-axis, representing the dependent variable.
How does the concept of Process: Graphical Communication help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Independent Axis
examples.content: The X-axis, representing the independent variable.

examples.title: Dependent Axis
examples.content: The Y-axis, representing the dependent variable.

slides.title: Process: Causal Inferences
slides.text:
Differentiating between correlation and causation when observing environmental phenomena.
This slide introduces key insights about Process: Causal Inferences.
For instance, consider the example of Causation:
One event directly producing the other (e.g., gravity causing objects to fall).
Another case is the study of Coincidence:
Two events happening together without a direct causal link.
How does the concept of Process: Causal Inferences help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Causation
examples.content: One event directly producing the other (e.g., gravity causing objects to fall).

examples.title: Coincidence
examples.content: Two events happening together without a direct causal link.

slides.title: Process: Kinetic Observations
slides.text:
Observing molecular activity in substances during phase transitions.
This slide introduces key insights about Process: Kinetic Observations.
For instance, consider the example of Kinetic Theory:
All matter consists of tiny particles in constant motion.
Another case is the study of Phase Transitions:
Heat changes particle motion, causing solid/liquid/gas shifts.
How does the concept of Process: Kinetic Observations help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Kinetic Theory
examples.content: All matter consists of tiny particles in constant motion.

examples.title: Phase Transitions
examples.content: Heat changes particle motion, causing solid/liquid/gas shifts.

slides.title: Process: Pressure Predictions
slides.text:
Predicting air pressure variations inside a closed cylinder when volume is compressed.
This slide introduces key insights about Process: Pressure Predictions.
For instance, consider the example of Boyle's Law:
Pressure of a gas is inversely proportional to its volume at constant temp.
Another case is the study of Prediction:
Decreasing volume by half will double the internal pressure.
How does the concept of Process: Pressure Predictions help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Boyle's Law
examples.content: Pressure of a gas is inversely proportional to its volume at constant temp.

examples.title: Prediction
examples.content: Decreasing volume by half will double the internal pressure.

slides.title: Process: Spatial Measurements
slides.text:
Measuring the coordinates of objects to map positions in three-dimensional space.
This slide introduces key insights about Process: Spatial Measurements.
For instance, consider the example of Cartesian Coordinates:
Using X, Y, and Z axes to define exact locations.
Another case is the study of Reference Point:
The fixed origin (0,0,0) from which all distances are measured.
How does the concept of Process: Spatial Measurements help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Cartesian Coordinates
examples.content: Using X, Y, and Z axes to define exact locations.

examples.title: Reference Point
examples.content: The fixed origin (0,0,0) from which all distances are measured.

slides.title: Process: Chemical Classification
slides.text:
Classifying household liquids into acids, bases, or neutrals using litmus paper.
This slide introduces key insights about Process: Chemical Classification.
For instance, consider the example of Indicator:
Chemicals that change color depending on pH levels.
Another case is the study of Litmus Paper:
Turns red in acids, blue in bases, and remains unchanged in neutrals.
How does the concept of Process: Chemical Classification help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Indicator
examples.content: Chemicals that change color depending on pH levels.

examples.title: Litmus Paper
examples.content: Turns red in acids, blue in bases, and remains unchanged in neutrals.

slides.title: Process: Geological Inferences
slides.text:
Inferring the history of an area by observing layers of rock and soil (stratigraphy).
This slide introduces key insights about Process: Geological Inferences.
For instance, consider the example of Law of Superposition:
Older rock layers are found at the bottom, younger at the top.
Another case is the study of Sedimentation:
The accumulation of minerals settling out of water over time.
How does the concept of Process: Geological Inferences help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Law of Superposition
examples.content: Older rock layers are found at the bottom, younger at the top.

examples.title: Sedimentation
examples.content: The accumulation of minerals settling out of water over time.

slides.title: Process: Cell Biology Observations
slides.text:
Observing cellular structures under a microscope during cell division (mitosis).
This slide introduces key insights about Process: Cell Biology Observations.
For instance, consider the example of Chromosomes:
Thread-like structures carrying genetic info that separate during division.
Another case is the study of Staining:
Adding dye to make cell structures visible under light.
How does the concept of Process: Cell Biology Observations help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Chromosomes
examples.content: Thread-like structures carrying genetic info that separate during division.

examples.title: Staining
examples.content: Adding dye to make cell structures visible under light.

slides.title: Process: Thermodynamic Predictions
slides.text:
Predicting the rate of cooling of a hot liquid based on insulation materials.
This slide introduces key insights about Process: Thermodynamic Predictions.
For instance, consider the example of Newton's Law of Cooling:
Rate of heat loss is proportional to temperature difference.
Another case is the study of Insulator:
Materials with low conductivity that slow down heat transfer.
How does the concept of Process: Thermodynamic Predictions help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Newton's Law of Cooling
examples.content: Rate of heat loss is proportional to temperature difference.

examples.title: Insulator
examples.content: Materials with low conductivity that slow down heat transfer.

slides.title: Process: Mass displacement
slides.text:
Measuring mass changes in a chemical reaction to verify conservation of mass.
This slide introduces key insights about Process: Mass displacement.
For instance, consider the example of Conservation of Mass:
Matter cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction.
Another case is the study of Closed System:
A sealed container preventing gas from escaping during tests.
How does the concept of Process: Mass displacement help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Conservation of Mass
examples.content: Matter cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction.

examples.title: Closed System
examples.content: A sealed container preventing gas from escaping during tests.

slides.title: Process: Ecological Classification
slides.text:
Classifying ecosystems into biomes based on average precipitation and temperature.
This slide introduces key insights about Process: Ecological Classification.
For instance, consider the example of Biome:
A large community of plants and animals occupying a distinct region.
Another case is the study of Climograph:
A graph showing monthly precipitation and temperature of a biome.
How does the concept of Process: Ecological Classification help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Biome
examples.content: A large community of plants and animals occupying a distinct region.

examples.title: Climograph
examples.content: A graph showing monthly precipitation and temperature of a biome.

slides.title: Guided Practice: Dichotomous Keys
slides.text:
Navigating a key to classify an unknown leaf specimen.
This slide introduces key insights about Guided Practice: Dichotomous Keys.
For instance, consider the example of Key Step 1:
Choose: Broad leaf (go to 2) or Needle-like leaf (Pine).
Another case is the study of Key Step 2:
Choose: Smooth edge (Oak) or Jagged edge (Maple).
How does the concept of Guided Practice: Dichotomous Keys help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Key Step 1
examples.content: Choose: Broad leaf (go to 2) or Needle-like leaf (Pine).

examples.title: Key Step 2
examples.content: Choose: Smooth edge (Oak) or Jagged edge (Maple).

slides.title: Guided Practice: Interpolating Data
slides.text:
Estimating value points between measured data markers on a graph.
This slide introduces key insights about Guided Practice: Interpolating Data.
For instance, consider the example of Method:
Find the value on the line between the plotted points.
Another case is the study of Accuracy:
Ensures data trend line is followed correctly.
How does the concept of Guided Practice: Interpolating Data help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Method
examples.content: Find the value on the line between the plotted points.

examples.title: Accuracy
examples.content: Ensures data trend line is followed correctly.

slides.title: Guided Practice: Extrapolating Trends
slides.text:
Extrapolating a growth curve beyond the measured time frame.
This slide introduces key insights about Guided Practice: Extrapolating Trends.
For instance, consider the example of Method:
Extend the trend line forward, maintaining the rate slope.
Another case is the study of Risk:
Assumes environmental factors remain constant in the future.
How does the concept of Guided Practice: Extrapolating Trends help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Method
examples.content: Extend the trend line forward, maintaining the rate slope.

examples.title: Risk
examples.content: Assumes environmental factors remain constant in the future.

slides.title: Guided Practice: Inferences from Fossils
slides.text:
Inferring ancient diets by observing fossilized tooth shapes.
This slide introduces key insights about Guided Practice: Inferences from Fossils.
For instance, consider the example of Fossil tooth:
Sharp, pointed teeth observed in fossil skull.
Another case is the study of Inference:
The animal was a carnivore (meat eater) because of tooth structure.
How does the concept of Guided Practice: Inferences from Fossils help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Fossil tooth
examples.content: Sharp, pointed teeth observed in fossil skull.

examples.title: Inference
examples.content: The animal was a carnivore (meat eater) because of tooth structure.

slides.title: Common Error: Mistaking Correlation for Causation
slides.text:
Assuming that because two trends rise together, one must cause the other.
This slide introduces key insights about Common Error: Mistaking Correlation for Causation.
For instance, consider the example of Incorrect:
Saying 'Ice cream sales cause sunburns because both rise in summer.'
Another case is the study of Correct:
The sun's heat causes both ice cream sales and sunburns.
How does the concept of Common Error: Mistaking Correlation for Causation help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Incorrect
examples.content: Saying 'Ice cream sales cause sunburns because both rise in summer.'

examples.title: Correct
examples.content: The sun's heat causes both ice cream sales and sunburns.

slides.title: Common Error: Cherry-Picking Data
slides.text:
Only selecting data points that confirm your hypothesis while ignoring exceptions.
This slide introduces key insights about Common Error: Cherry-Picking Data.
For instance, consider the example of Incorrect:
Recording only the fast car runs and ignoring the slow ones.
Another case is the study of Correct:
Calculating the average of all trials, including anomalies.
How does the concept of Common Error: Cherry-Picking Data help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Incorrect
examples.content: Recording only the fast car runs and ignoring the slow ones.

examples.title: Correct
examples.content: Calculating the average of all trials, including anomalies.

slides.title: Summary of Week 4 Advanced
slides.text:
You completed Week 4 Advanced! You now possess a complete toolkit of science process skills.
This slide introduces key insights about Summary of Week 4 Advanced.
For instance, consider the example of Key Takeaway:
Process skills are integrated to build models and establish laws.
Another case is the study of Next Step:
Review your learnings and prepare for the term culminating tasks!
How does the concept of Summary of Week 4 Advanced help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Key Takeaway
examples.content: Process skills are integrated to build models and establish laws.

examples.title: Next Step
examples.content: Review your learnings and prepare for the term culminating tasks!

standard.q: Which process skill involves estimating values between observed data points on a graph?
What do we call the morphological characteristic key used to identify organisms?
According to Archimedes' principle, what force keeps a boat floating?
options: Buoyant force equal to the displaced water weight

standard.q: Which of the following is a scientific inference based on superposed rock layers?
options: The bottom layer was deposited earlier than the top layer.
The rock layer is 3 meters thick.

standard.q: What does the Law of Conservation of Mass state about chemical reactions?
options: Mass is neither created nor destroyed in a closed system.
Mass turns into energy and disappears.

standard.q: True or False: Correlation always proves that one variable caused the change in another.
Which axis on a standard line graph represents the independent variable?
What is the term for estimating values BEYOND the measured data range?
Why do scientists use a dichotomous key?
options: To systematically identify organisms based on physical characteristics

standard.q: What color does blue litmus paper turn when dipped in an acid?
True or False: Boyle's Law states that gas pressure increases as volume decreases at constant temperature.
Which type of heat transfer occurs through fluid currents?
What is the meniscus point for reading water volume in glass cylinders?
options: The bottom of the concave curve

standard.q: What plant structures open and close to regulate gas exchange?
Why do we stain cells before viewing them under a light microscope?
options: To increase contrast and make cellular structures visible

standard.q: Which of the following is a physical property of a base?
True or False: Newton's Law of Cooling states that cooling rate depends on the temperature difference between object and surroundings.
What organic layer of soil holds nutrients and water for agriculture?
Which tool measures atmospheric pressure trends?
True or False: Extrapolating data carries the risk of assuming trends continue indefinitely without change.
What happens to water density when it freezes into ice?
options: It decreases, which is why ice floats.

standard.q: Which of the following is a morphological characteristic of a leaf?
options: The serrated or smooth edge structure

standard.q: True or False: A closed system prevents mass exchange with the outside environment.
What hormone causes phototropism by gathering on the shaded side of stems?
Which process skill is main tool for identifying rock age in geologic layers?
options: Inferring layer sequence based on superposition laws

challenge.q: A student plots water cooling rates. At 0 mins temp is 90C; at 5 mins it is 70C; at 10 mins it is 55C. What is the interpolated temperature at 7 minutes?
You are using a dichotomous key to identify a tree. The key states: 1a. Needle-like leaves (Pine); 1b. Broad leaves (go to 2). The specimen has broad leaves. What is the next step?
options: Proceed to step 2 in the key.

challenge.q: Why does a closed jar containing vinegar and baking soda weigh the same before and after they react?
options: Mass is conserved because no atoms could escape the sealed container.

challenge.q: What type of error is committed if a weather model predicts sunny weather based on a trend but ignores an incoming low-pressure front?
options: Extrapolation error due to ignoring changing variables

challenge.q: An ecologist wants to classify a biome. The climograph shows low precipitation and high temperatures year-round. What is the classification?

performance.title: Archimedes' Buoyancy & Density Investigation
performance.desc: Select three solid objects (e.g., a plastic toy, a metal bolt, a piece of wood). Measure the mass of each object. Build a water displacement cup to measure the volume of displaced water for each. Calculate the density of each object, write down a prediction about their buoyancy, and explain how the buoyant force relates to water displacement to your parent.
labels: Accurately measured mass and displaced water volume
Correctly calculated density using the mass/volume formula
Wrote a clear, scientific explanation of buoyant force based on Archimedes' principle

pages:
Exercise 1: Dichotomous Key Navigation

Use the dichotomous key below to identify the animal specimen: A vertebrate with feathers and wings.

1a. Has backbone (go to 2) | 1b. No backbone (Invertebrate)
2a. Has feathers (Bird) | 2b. No feathers (go to 3)
What is the classification of the specimen?
Bird

Exercise 2: Interpolation & Extrapolation

Read the graph data table and write the estimated values.

Time (minutes) 0 2 4 6 (estimated)
Plant Height (cm) 10 12 14 16
Was finding the height at 6 minutes an example of interpolation or extrapolation?
Extrapolation

Exercise 3: Conservation of Mass

Verify the law of conservation of mass using experimental values.

Scenario: 10g of baking soda reacts with 50g of vinegar in a closed flask. A gas forms.
1. What is the total mass of the flask contents after the chemical reaction?
60g
2. What scientific law justifies this answer?
Law of Conservation of Mass
